title: Applications of monotone constraint satisfaction
speaker: Robert Robere
abstract: Recently, a certain "monotone" version of the constraint satisfaction problem has proved an extremely useful tool for attacking problems in circuit, communication, and proof complexity theory. In this talk we discuss this version of the constraint satisfaction problem and touch on its connection to fundamental lower-bounds problems in these areas. We also consider a recent and interesting application: the first exponential lower bounds on the length of cutting planes refutations of random CNF formulas.

proof_stages.label: Proof-complexity lower-bound program
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames propositional proof complexity as the study of proof length for tautologies, recalls the NP versus coNP motivation, surveys proof systems, and identifies known lower-bound techniques and their reach.

proof_stages.label: Feasible interpolation bottleneck
proof_stages.summary: Feasible interpolation is described as converting refutations of split formulas into comparable-size circuits for an interpolating Boolean function, explaining why existing cutting-planes lower bounds only handled formulas of this special split form.

proof_stages.label: Random-CNF cutting-planes goal and monotone-circuit strategy
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated as exponential lower bounds for cutting-planes refutations of random SAT instances, with the strategy of passing through an RCC proof system equivalent to real monotone circuits computing an associated monotone CSP function.

proof_stages.label: Communication proofs and proof-to-search conversion
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines CC/RCC-style semantic proofs whose lines have low communication complexity and shows how such a proof yields a DAG-like protocol for the canonical search problem of finding a falsified clause.

proof_stages.label: Monotone CSP and the Raz--McKenzie/Karchmer--Wigderson bridge
proof_stages.summary: A monotone CSP function is constructed by fixing the constraint topology and taking truth tables as inputs, and the Karchmer--Wigderson theorem together with the Raz--McKenzie reduction identifies its monotone circuit size with the communication search problem arising from the CNF.

proof_stages.label: Real-protocol lift and cutting-planes simulation
proof_stages.summary: The bit-communication equivalence is lifted to real communication protocols and real monotone circuits, using known results and the fact that the corresponding RCC system simulates cutting planes.

proof_stages.label: Random-CNF monotone lower bound via symmetric approximations
proof_stages.summary: To complete the lower bound, the talk invokes the symmetric method of approximations for monotone circuits and explains that, for random CNFs, the required scattering parameters are controlled by expansion properties.

proof_stages.label: Consequences, limitations, and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion emphasizes the first exponential cutting-planes lower bounds beyond split interpolation formulas, then discusses obstacles for nonmonotone circuits, size lifting, other formula families, and the need for more direct cutting-planes techniques.

examples.name: Landscape of Boolean, algebraic, and semialgebraic proof systems
examples.note: The speaker lists familiar Boolean systems, algebraic systems such as Nullstellensatz-style systems, and semialgebraic systems to illustrate the range of proof systems in which lower bounds are studied.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker surveys the proof-system landscape and states where lower bounds/interpolation apply, but does not compute or run an argument on the landscape itself
